sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=19, col=48).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 27, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 26 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Now clicking red has a different effect. The left panel now shows:
- Blue block at rows 20-23, cols 18-21 (top-right)
- RED block at rows 24-27, cols 18-21 (below blue block, vertical)
- Dark red block at rows 30-33, cols 18-21 (below red block, vertical)
- Gray block at rows 30-33, cols 8-11 (left side)
- Checkerboard at rows 34-37, cols 8-11 (below gray block)
- Green piece at rows 40-41, cols 10-11 (in container)
So the red block moved from its horizontal position (rows 30-33, cols 12-17) to a vertical position (rows 24-27, cols 18-21), stacking below the blue block! And the dark red block also moved to be below the red block (rows 30-33, cols 18-21).
Wait, but the gray block is now at rows 30-33, cols 8-11. Let me re-examine...
Actually looking more carefully:
- Row 20-23: ccccccccccccbbbb (blue block at cols 18-21)
- Row 24-27: ccccccccccccRRRR (red block at cols 18-21)
- Row 30-33: ccggggccccccrrrr (gray at cols 8-11, dark red at cols 18-21)
- Row 34-37: checkerboard at cols 8-11
So the red block moved vertically and is now stacked below the blue block. The dark red block is at rows 30-33, cols 18-21. The gray block is at rows 30-33, cols 8-11.
This is a new arrangement! Previously clicking red would toggle the red block between horizontal and vertical positions. Now it seems to have stacked the red block below the blue block.
So the clicking behavior depends on the current state of the blocks. This is getting more complex. Let me track all the states I've seen:
State 0 (initial): 
- Blue block top-right (20-23, 18-21)
- Gray block middle-left (30-33, 8-11)
- Red block middle-center (30-33, 12-17)
- Dark red block middle-right (30-33, 18-21)
- Checkerboard bottom-left (34-37, 8-11)
- Characters outlined
State 1 (after first red click):
- Red block moved to vertical position (24-29, 18-21)
- Characters filled
State 2 (after blue click):
- Blue block moved to bottom-left (34-37, 8-11)
- Checkerboard moved to top-right (20-23, 18-21)
State 3 (after second red click):
- Red block back to horizontal (30-33, 12-17)
- Blue block back to top-right (20-23, 18-21)
- Checkerboard back to bottom-left (34-37, 8-11)
State 4 (after second blue click):
- Same as State 3 (blocks back to initial positions)
- Characters still filled
State 5 (current, after third red click):
- Blue block at top-right (20-23, 18-21)
- Red block at rows 24-27, cols 18-21 (below blue)
- Dark red block at rows 30-33, cols 18-21
- Gray block at rows 30-33, cols 8-11
- Checkerboard at rows 34-37, cols 8-11
So clicking red when blocks are in initial arrangement (but characters filled) causes a different transformation than the first time. The red block stacks vertically below the blue block.
This is a puzzle where clicking characters rearranges blocks. The goal might be to stack all blocks in a specific configuration.
Looking at the current arrangement, the blocks are now stacked vertically on the right side: blue, red, dark red. This looks like it could be building toward a target configuration.
Let me think about what the target might be. The character shapes on the right are like "person" shapes. Maybe the goal is to stack the blocks to form a similar shape on the left?
Let me try clicking blue to see what happens next.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# New state: red block stacked vertically below blue block
# Blue (20-23, 18-21), Red (24-27, 18-21), Dark red (30-33, 18-21)
# Gray (30-33, 8-11), Checkerboard (34-37, 8-11)
# Characters still filled

# Let me try clicking blue to see what happens
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 48}])
print("Click blue: board_changed =", result.get('board_changed'))

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Left panel ===")
for i in range(14, 44):
    row_str = ascii_lines[i][6:22]
    if any(c not in 'c' for c in row_str):
        print(f"  {i}: {row_str}")

print("\n=== Right panel ===")
for i in range(14, 44):
    row_str = ascii_lines[i][34:60]
    if any(c not in 'B' for c in row_str):
        print(f"  {i}: {row_str}")

print("\nLevel completed:", result.get('level_completed'))
print("Done:", result.get('done'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
